stories.title: Law Firms, AI Company Sign Leases at Elecor’s One Market Plaza
stories.excerpt: Elecor Properties, rebranded from Paramount Group, has secured four leases totaling 149,914 square feet at the two-building One Market Plaza in San Francisco since the beginning of the year. The leases follow Elecor’s announcement of a $250-million investment initiative across its portfolio in New York and San Francisco. The transactions include a 42,445-square-foot lease by global law firm Davis Polk & Wardwell LLP, and a 33,566-square-foot lease by Willkie Farr & Gallagher LLP. Another global law firm expanded its existing footprint by 11,519 square feet to occupy a full floor and an AI company committed to 62,384 square feet spanning three floors. “One Market Plaza combines an iconic waterfront location, exceptional connectivity and large-block availability, complemented by a transformative capital improvement program designed to deliver a truly elevated workplace experience,” said Peter Brindley, EVP and head of real estate at Elecor. Elecor Properties is advancing a series of planned enhancements at One Market Plaza designed to further elevate the tenant experience. Upcoming improvements include a reimagined atrium and ground-floor arrival experience, a state-of-the-art conferencing center, upgraded fitness facility, atrium bar, executive lounge, rooftop deck and a seventh-floor sky bar. JLL’s Chris Roeder, Ted Davies, Tom Doupe, Wes Powell and Teva Myatt represented Elecor Properties in the transactions. The post Law Firms, AI Company Sign Leases at Elecor’s One Market Plaza appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Davis Polk, Willkie Farr, AI company lease 149.9K SF at One Market Plaza SF since Jan 2026.
Elecor investing $250M across NY/SF portfolio; amenities upgrade (atrium, sky bar, conferencing) targets modern office tenant.
Large-block availability and waterfront positioning signal SF office market stabilization and tenant demand for premium space.
stories.summary: Elecor Properties (formerly Paramount Group) has secured four office leases totaling 149,914 sq ft at One Market Plaza in San Francisco since January 2026, including major commitments from law firms Davis Polk (42,445 SF), Willkie Farr (33,566 SF), and an unnamed AI company (62,384 SF). The leasing follows Elecor's announcement of a $250 million investment initiative across New York and San Francisco properties. The landlord is undertaking a capital improvement program including a reimagined atrium, state-of-the-art conferencing center, fitness upgrade, and rooftop/sky bar amenities designed to elevate tenant experience.
stories.impact: Large corporate and professional service tenants committing to premium San Francisco office space at scale signals rental-demand stabilization in the Bay Area, suggesting the office-to-residential conversion panic may be overblown in prime waterfront submarkets. This validates continued investment in mixed-use development in SF's core submarkets where institutional tenants remain willing to pay for modern, amenitized office. For multifamily developers, this indicates residual corporate headcount and wage growth in San Francisco that supports sustained apartment demand in proximity to downtown markets.

stories.title: Isaac Toledano, Israeli investor’s dueling real estate lawsuits spill into family drama
stories.excerpt: Isaac Toledano is tied up in dueling lawsuits with an Israeli businessman over South Florida real estate in a dispute that has boiled over into family drama tied to one of Toledano’s daughters.  Yaakov Morad and his affiliate Apirion Real Estate sued Toledano on June 23, alleging that Toledano lied about the value of a 28,000-square-foot Aventura office building and then flipped it to Morad and Apirion at an “inflated” price, according to the complaint. Toledano used Morad’s limited English and the fact he was doing the deal as part of a 1031 exchange to pressure him through the due […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Isaac Toledano (BH Group) expanding South Florida portfolio amid dueling lawsuits over Aventura office, Cutler Bay mixed-use.
Litigation involves 28K SF Aventura office and Southland City Center (Cutler Bay) mixed-use; personal disputes tied to capital calls.
Toledano now prolific South Florida developer partnering with Related Group; legal volatility does not impede deal flow.
stories.summary: Isaac Toledano, an Israeli-American developer via his Aventura-based BH Group, is embroiled in dueling lawsuits with Israeli businessman Yaakov Morad over a 28,000 sq ft Aventura office building (alleging inflated sale price) and the Southland City Center mixed-use project in Cutler Bay (with Morad allegedly $720K+ behind on capital obligations). Toledano counersued, denying wrongdoing and claiming the office sale was below market. The dispute has spilled into family drama tied to Morad's brother's marriage to one of Toledano's daughters.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact to multifamily development market. Litigation between co-developers on a mixed-use Cutler Bay project creates execution risk on that asset, but Toledano's broader trajectory—from relative obscurity four years ago to prolific South Florida developer partnering with institutional players like Related Group—suggests personal legal entanglements have not materially constrained his ability to acquire, finance, and develop. The dispute is a counterparty/credit signal, not a market signal.

stories.title: Charney Companies Buys 143 Roebling Street in Williamsburg for $20M
stories.excerpt: Prolific developer Charney Companies has acquired a historic residential building in Williamsburg, Brooklyn, Commercial Observer has learned. Charney bought 143 Roebling Street, a five-story, 49-unit loft and rental property in Williamsburg, from Los Angeles-based institutional lender Calmwater Capital for $20 million, according to the buyer. Charney intends to restore the building, which has been vacant for several years. Built in 1907, 143 Roebling has served as both a residential and retail property and once housed the popular Roebling Tea Room. “We are very excited to be taking over ownership for this building and know local residents will be as well,” Justin Pelsinger, chief operating officer for Charney, said in a statement. “We are looking forward to restoring and modernizing this neighborhood gem to better serve Brooklynites for generations to come.” BH3 Fund Advisors provided $30 million in financing for the acquisition, predevelopment and planning of the project. “Charney has a history of delivering exceptional projects in Brooklyn and beyond, and this asset is now in the hands of a sponsorship team capable of unlocking its potential after being dormant for the better part of a decade,” Adam Falk, principal and co-portfolio manager at BH3, said in a statement. “Charney’s track record, coupled with BH3’s experience in New York City, will help ensure the property catches up to the surrounding neighborhood.” Calmwater Capital, previously a lender to former 143 Roebling owner RedSky Capital, took over the building at the corner of Metropolitan Avenue through a deed in lieu of foreclosure transaction in January 2022 valued at $82.7 million, property records show. Calmwater Capital was represented in the new deal by Ethan Stanton, Brendan Maddigan and Michael Mazzara from JLL. The brokerage firm worked directly with Charney Companies. JLL did not immediately respond to CO’s request for comment. Founded in 2013, Charney Companies has a diverse portfolio as a deve
bullets: Charney Companies acquires 143 Roebling Street (49-unit historic loft) in Williamsburg for $20M from Calmwater Capital.
Building vacant for years; Charney plans restoration; $30M financing from BH3 Fund Advisors for acquisition and predevelopment.
Williamsburg development activity remains robust; lender (Calmwater) divesting non-performing or legacy assets, freeing capital.
stories.summary: Prolific developer Charney Companies has acquired 143 Roebling Street, a five-story, 49-unit historic loft and rental property in Williamsburg, Brooklyn, from LA-based lender Calmwater Capital for $20 million. The 1907-built asset has been vacant for several years and formerly housed the Roebling Tea Room. Charney intends to restore and modernize the building. BH3 Fund Advisors provided $30 million in financing for acquisition, predevelopment, and planning.
stories.impact: Charney's acquisition of a long-vacant Williamsburg asset signals ongoing developer appetite for value-add repositioning in Brooklyn's prime neighborhoods, despite headline headlines about slowing development. The lender's divestiture (Calmwater) suggests institutional lenders are still willing to offload non-core or problem assets to operational developers, indicating a functioning secondary market for legacy/troubled units. For multifamily developers, this validates Williamsburg as a market where capital and operational expertise can unlock value from dormant inventory, supporting continued development and rent growth in the submarket.

stories.title: The Bond Market Is Taking the Fed’s Hawkish Cue
stories.excerpt: Executive Summary Kevin Warsh’s arrival as Fed chair is reinforcing a more hawkish tone and a deliberate shift toward letting markets take the lead in pricing interest rates. Bond investors appear to be accepting that invitation. Real Treasury yields have climbed to multi-year highs, the yield curve has flattened sharply, overnight index swaps have repriced toward a higher policy path, and mortgage spreads remain historically wide. Warsh’s ‘Market-Led’ Policy and Real Yields Warsh, in his first press conference, highlighted that inflation has persistently run above the Fed’s 2% goal for over five years, describing high prices as a burden on Americans. He expressed comfort with markets taking a more active role: “The more that markets are paying attention to what’s happening in the real economy… the more financial markets can price what they believe is the most likely and what the tail risks are.” Markets have responded. The 5‑year TIPS yield now trades around 2.0% to 2.03%, its first sustained move through that threshold in over a year and roughly 90 basis points higher than levels seen in late February. The 10‑year real yield is also above 2%, and the long end of the curve is higher still, with 30‑year TIPS yields around 2.6% to 2.7%. Recent auctions tell the same story: a mid‑June reopening of 5‑year TIPS cleared at 1.955%, well above the 1.367% real yield from the original April issue of the same security. Warsh has said “financial markets perform best when they react to incoming data,” and hotter headline inflation since the war with Iran, particularly via energy prices, has lifted real yields as investors reprice both the baseline and tail risks for future policy. Swaps, OAS and MBS: How Markets are Repricing Policy The hawkish shift is visible beyond TIPS. In the derivatives markets, US 5‑year swap rates have moved up to the low‑4% area, around 4.17–4.19% in late June, reflecting higher expectations for the Fed’s medium‑term policy rate. The 5‑year Treasury no
bullets: Fed Chair Kevin Warsh signals hawkish stance; 5-year TIPS yield near 2.0%, 90 bps higher than Feb 2026.
Real yields climb to multi-year highs; 10-year real yield above 2%; 30-year TIPS at 2.6%–2.7%.
Mortgage spreads remain historically wide; markets repricing policy path higher amid persistent inflation above Fed's 2% target.
stories.summary: Fed Chair Kevin Warsh has signaled a more hawkish tone and a deliberate shift toward letting financial markets price interest rates. Real Treasury yields have climbed to multi-year highs, with 5-year TIPS yields trading near 2.0%–2.03% (roughly 90 basis points higher than late February levels) and 10-year real yields above 2%. Mortgage spreads remain historically wide. Markets are repricing both baseline and tail risks for future Fed policy amid persistent inflation running above the Fed's 2% goal for over five years.
stories.impact: Rising real yields and wider mortgage spreads directly compress debt service capacity and cap-rate expansion for multifamily development underwriting. Higher real cost of capital raises the hurdle rate for new development starts and refinances, particularly in secondary/tertiary markets with thinner spreads to risk-free rates. Developers will face tighter underwriting, smaller feasible rent assumptions, and reduced construction financing availability. This is the primary headwind to starts through 2026–2027; projects underwritten at lower cap rates (sub-4.5%) in late 2024–2025 will struggle to secure takeout financing. Cap rates will likely rise 50–100 bps before equilibrium returns.

stories.title: Simon Property Group Wants to Build Mixed-Use Dev at Boca Raton Mall
stories.excerpt: Simon Property Group wants to expand its 1.7 million-square-foot indoor mall in Boca Raton, Fla., by replacing a shuttered Sears department store with a mixed-use development featuring housing and a hotel. The proposal would span roughly 32 acres adjacent to Simon’s Town Center at Boca Raton shopping mall, replacing a parking lot and the former Sears at 5900 West Glades Road. The Indianapolis-based mall operator purchased the Sears property for $23 million in 2025. The department store, which closed its Boca Raton outpost in 2018, would be demolished. Plans call for the construction of an eight-story, 197-room hotel; a seven-story, 374-unit rental building; eight new retail buildings totaling 157,170 square feet; and an eight-story, 639-spot parking garage. “This proposed transformation of a long-vacant space into a vibrant mixed-use destination will introduce an exceptional collection of luxury and premium retail and first to market dining offerings that further elevate the property’s position as South Florida’s premier luxury shopping and lifestyle destination,” Mark Silvestri, president of development at Simon Property Group, said in a statement. Boca Raton officials have been reviewing the application since May, the South Florida Business Journal first reported. A representative for Simon did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The proposal comes about eight months after Simon and partner Turnberry paid $131 million to purchase the 219,000-square-foot, open-air property from Seritage Growth Properties, the real estate investment trust launched after Sears declared bankruptcy and closed almost all of its stores. The REIT turned the shuttered department store attached to the Aventura Mall into a mixed-use development, which houses the office of Palantir, the controversial analytics software company. Further expanding its South Florida footprint, Simon bought the mall at Brickell City Centre in Miami for $512 million last
bullets: Simon Property Group plans 374-unit rental + 197-room hotel on 32-acre Boca Raton mixed-use development.
Site replaces long-vacant Sears (closed 2018); Simon purchased Sears for $23M in 2025; total project cost ~$197M ($45M+ for retail phase).
South Florida real estate capital shifting from traditional retail to mixed-use; vacant mall anchor conversions accelerating.
stories.summary: Simon Property Group plans to develop a mixed-use project spanning roughly 32 acres adjacent to its 1.7 million sq ft Town Center at Boca Raton mall, replacing a shuttered Sears and a parking lot at 5900 West Glades Road. The development includes a 374-unit rental building, 197-room hotel, 157,170 sq ft of retail across eight buildings, and an 639-spot parking garage. Simon purchased the Sears property for $23 million in 2025; the project is expected to deliver by end of 2027.
stories.impact: Simon's conversion of vacant anchor retail into mixed-use residential signals a structural shift in mall operator strategy in South Florida—trading single-tenant anchor dependency for diversified income streams (multifamily + hospitality). This validates the long-term fundamentals of Boca Raton as a growth market (affluent, coastal, strong demand) and creates a template for other mall operators to follow. For multifamily developers in South Florida, this represents direct capital competition (Simon has balance sheet and tax-efficient capital) but also validates rental demand in Boca Raton's core retail-anchored nodes, supporting pro-forma assumptions for new construction or value-add in the submarket.

stories.title: Shorenstein Relocates HQ to 6K SF at 45 East 53rd Street
stories.excerpt: Real estate owner and operator Shorenstein is departing its longtime home at 850 Third Avenue for a full-floor lease at 45 East 53rd Street, Commercial Observer has learned. The company, also known as Shorenstein Properties, has signed a 5,560-square-foot deal to relocate its regional headquarters to the 20-story tower, known as Park 53. The seven-year lease with landlords ZG Capital Partners and Rialto Capital Management secures Shorenstein a prebuilt space across the entire 16th floor. Asking rents at the Midtown building, located between Park and Madison avenues, range from $125 to $155 per square foot. The 140,000-square-foot office property once served as Santander Bank’s U.S. headquarters. ZG Capital and Rialto Capital acquired the tower for $105 million in 2022, and undertook a Class A repositioning. “Welcoming a firm like Shorenstein validates that strategy and reinforces Park 53’s position as a premier Midtown address,” Bobby Zar, head of ZG Capital, said in a statement. JLL’s Mitch Konsker, Benjamin Bass, Alex Chudnoff, Greg Wang and Thomas Swartz represented the landlords, while CBRE’s Sacha Zarba, Kyle Zarba and Claire Pisano negotiated on behalf of Shorenstein. Other tenants at Park 53 include mortgage lenders Acres Capital and CrossCountry Mortgage, as well as asset management company Bramshill Investments. It is unclear why or when Shorenstein plans to make the roughly three-block leap east from 850 Third Avenue, but the ultimate departure may be just a little bittersweet. Shorenstein owned the 21-story Third Avenue tower from 2008 to 2016, when it was acquired by a joint venture for $463 million. Current owners HPS Investment Partners and Waterman Interests are currently undertaking a repositioning of the 617,000-square-foot office building. A spokesperson for Shorenstein declined to comment. A CBRE spokesperson did not respond to a request for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Shorenstein Properties relocates HQ from 850 Third Avenue to 5.56K SF at Park 53 (45 East 53rd Street, Midtown).
Seven-year lease at $125–$155/sf; 16th floor, full-floor prebuilt space in 20-story tower acquired by ZG Capital/Rialto for $105M in 2022.
Asset recapitalization validates Midtown office repositioning; flight from legacy buildings to Class A continue.
stories.summary: Real estate operator Shorenstein Properties has relocated its regional headquarters from its longtime home at 850 Third Avenue to a 5,560 sq ft full-floor lease at Park 53 (45 East 53rd Street) in Midtown Manhattan. The seven-year lease was negotiated at asking rents of $125–$155 per square foot for the entire 16th floor of the 20-story, 140,000 sq ft tower. ZG Capital Partners and Rialto Capital Management own the property, which they acquired for $105 million in 2022 and repositioned as Class A office space (formerly Santander's U.S. HQ).
stories.impact: Shorenstein's move to Park 53 validates ZG Capital/Rialto's Class A repositioning strategy in Midtown and signals tenant-driven flight from legacy buildings to modernized space with lower obsolescence risk. For multifamily developers, this confirms ongoing office-to-residential pressure in legacy Midtown buildings (850 Third Avenue will likely face conversion pressures or value-dilution), but underscores that trophy Midtown assets remain rentable at institutional-grade rents when repositioned. The deal is less a signal of broad Midtown strength and more a marker that prime space remains competitive while secondary/tertiary office faces structural decline.

stories.title: Lender Offloads 301-Unit Tucson Apartment Asset
stories.excerpt: A lender-owned Tucson apartment complex traded for $32 million, well below what the previous buyer paid for the development four years earlier. Multihousing News reports that Trez Capital divested the asset, Peaks at Redington, following the previous owner’s deed-in-lieu-of-foreclosure in January 2026. The latest transaction’s sale price represents a 40% discount to the property’s previous trade four years ago, which was $53.9 million. Greenwater Real Estate Management was the buyer of the discounted property. MF Asset Management previously purchased the property in 2022, financing the acquisition with a $44.6 million loan from Trez. Constructed in 1980, Peaks at Redington features one- and two-bedroom units averaging 722 square feet. Clint Wadlund and Hamid Panahi of IPA Advisors represented the seller in the disposition of the lender-owned asset and procured Greenwater. The post Lender Offloads 301-Unit Tucson Apartment Asset appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Trez Capital sells Peaks at Redington (301-unit, 1980-built Tucson community) for $32M after deed-in-lieu from MF Asset Management.
Sale represents 40% discount to 2022 trade ($53.9M); prior 2022 financing was $44.6M from Trez; Greenwater Real Estate acquired discounted asset.
Aging garden-style asset underperforming; lender losses and distressed disposition signal Tucson market weakness or operator challenges.
stories.summary: Trez Capital divested Peaks at Redington, a 301-unit garden-style apartment complex in Tucson, for $32 million following a deed-in-lieu-of-foreclosure by the prior owner (MF Asset Management) in January 2026. The sale price represents a 40% discount to the property's prior sale in 2022 for $53.9 million. MF Asset had financed the 2022 acquisition with a $44.6 million Trez Capital loan. Greenwater Real Estate Management was the buyer. The property is a 1980-built community with one- and two-bedroom units averaging 722 sq ft.
stories.impact: Lender-forced sale of 1980-built garden-style apartment at 40% discount signals underwriting deterioration or operator performance failure in Tucson market. Debt service assumptions from 2022 were likely predicated on rent growth and low-cost operations that failed to materialize, creating a loss for both operator and lender. For multifamily developers, this flags Tucson as a market with potential rent pressure (aging supply, limited demographic growth relative to Sunbelt peers) and suggests underwriting discipline on garden-style assets with limited amenity/unit renovation upside. The distressed pricing may create acquisition opportunities for disciplined operators, but indicates capital discipline and lower-risk underwriting going forward.

stories.title: On Rejection and Perseverance at the NHL Draft
stories.excerpt: This past weekend, I took my wife, Cynthia, and my daughter, Sophie, to the National Hockey League draft in Buffalo. We are all huge hockey fans, and this past year Sophie served on the NHL’s Youth Advisory Board, so attending the draft was really the icing on the cake. We arrived early enough to watch many of the top prospects walk the red carpet. It was an incredible scene. Young men who had spent their entire lives chasing one dream were surrounded by parents, siblings, grandparents and friends, all hoping to witness a moment they had imagined for years. Every selection brought hugs, tears, smiles and overwhelming emotion as these talented players heard their names called and realized they were headed to the NHL. It was impossible not to feel happy for them. Robert Knakal. PHOTO: Patrick McMullan/Patrick McMullan via Getty Images The first round on Friday night took nearly four hours. Saturday consisted of rounds two through seven, lasting from 11 in the morning until about 5 in the afternoon. As the day wore on, the crowd slowly became smaller. Cynthia and I eventually returned to the hotel, but Sophie and a couple of her friends stayed until the very last selection. Then my phone buzzed. Sophie sent me a photograph that immediately stopped me in my tracks. It wasn’t of one of the players celebrating with his family. It was of a young man sitting completely alone in a suit and tie, patiently waiting for his name to be called. The arena was nearly empty by then, making his isolation even more striking. Sophie told me she and her friends felt terrible for him. By the end of the draft, 224 players had been selected. He wasn’t one of them. I couldn’t stop thinking about him. What happens next? This may very well be one of the biggest inflection points of his life. Does he conclude that his hockey dream is over and choose another path? Perhaps he discovers a new passion, builds a successful career, meets the love of his life, raises a wonderfu
bullets: Article covers 2026 NHL Draft in Buffalo; personal narrative about rejection, perseverance, and youth sports.
No real estate, development, or market-relevant content; unrelated to multifamily capital, supply, demand, or cost drivers.
stories.summary: A personal essay reflecting on attending the 2026 NHL Draft in Buffalo with family and reflecting on themes of rejection, perseverance, and youth ambition among drafted players.
stories.impact: No relevance to multifamily real estate development, capital markets, or apartment supply/demand dynamics. This article appears to be editorial or lifestyle content included in a real estate publication; it has no market signal or implication for multifamily developers.

stories.title: Booming Buckeye Slated for 114K-SF Retail Center
stories.excerpt: Retail developers count rooftops, and Buckeye is one of the nation’s fastest-growing cities. Buckeye’s population has increased by over 41% since the 2020 census to over 131,000. The Pederson Group is planning to build an over 114,000-square-foot retail complex anchored by Safeway at the northeast corner of Canyon Springs Boulevard and Sun Valley Parkway within the Festival Ranch master-planned community. Pederson purchased the 15-acre site where it plans to build the new shopping center from Festival Ranch’s developer, PulteGroup, for $2.5 million. Festival Ranch Marketplace is expected to open at the end of 2027 and will include other retail, dining and service tenants. Butler Design Group is the architect. Pederson’s expected development costs for Festival Ranch Marketplace are $45 million. Phoenix Commercial Advisors’ Zach Pace and Cameron Warren are handling leasing. The post Booming Buckeye Slated for 114K-SF Retail Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Buckeye, AZ population grew 41% since 2020 census to 131K; Pederson Group building 114.5K SF retail center anchored by Safeway.
Festival Ranch Marketplace at northeast corner Canyon Springs/Sun Valley Parkway; $45M project cost; opening end 2027.
One of nation's fastest-growing metros; retail developers follow demographic tailwinds; rooftop opportunity for residential near retail anchors.
stories.summary: Buckeye, Arizona, one of the nation's fastest-growing cities, has seen its population surge 41% since the 2020 census to over 131,000. The Pederson Group is planning Festival Ranch Marketplace, a 114,000+ sq ft retail center anchored by Safeway at the northeast corner of Canyon Springs Boulevard and Sun Valley Parkway within the Festival Ranch master-planned community. Pederson purchased the 15-acre site from PulteGroup for $2.5 million; project development costs are estimated at $45 million, with opening targeted for end of 2027.
stories.impact: Buckeye's 41% population growth since 2020 is one of the highest in the U.S. and signals strong in-migration and household formation—primary demand drivers for multifamily development. The presence of a master-planned community (Festival Ranch) with infrastructure and retail anchors (Safeway-anchored center) validates planning and employment accessibility. For multifamily developers, Buckeye represents a Sunbelt acquisition opportunity with demographic tailwinds, likely lower land costs than Phoenix proper, and potential institutional capital appetite for new-build in fast-growth exurban markets. The retail center is a leading indicator of larger mixed-use/residential feasibility in the Festival Ranch node.

stories.title: CMBS Loan Backed by Moinian Group’s 2 Washington Street Sent to Special Servicing
stories.excerpt: One of the Moinian Group’s premier properties is now in the hands of special servicing. A $131.5 million commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) loan was secured by 2 Washington Street, a 483,000-square-foot, 22-story mixed-use complex near the southern tip of Manhattan that leased short-term rental units. Cash flow issues led to the CMBS loan’s transfer to special servicing, according to an alert from Morningstar Credit Analytics. The $131.5 million loan is split among BMARK 2021-B29, BMARK 2021-B28 and 3650R 2021-PF1 conduit deals. The loans are set to mature on Aug. 6, 2031, according to Morningstar Credit. BMARK 2021-B29 and BMARK 2021-B28 are conduit deals issued by Citigroup and Bank of America in 2021, while the 3650R 2021-PF1 was securitized by 3650 Capital that same year. 2 Washington Street’s cash flow problems stem from its master-lease tenant, Sonder, a global hospitality company that sold hybrid hotel-apartment units featuring fully furnished rooms, keyless digital entry, and in-unit kitchens managed by Sonder staff. The short-term rental company began occupying 2 Washington Street — a 1970s-era office that was converted in the 2010s into residences — in late 2020. The firm entered into bankruptcy in November 2025, shortly after Marriott terminated a licensing deal, putting the firm’s roughly 9,000 rental units in more than 40 cities in limbo once its business model collapsed. “Sonder went bankrupt and ceased operations in November 2025, effectively cutting off the majority of the cash flow at the property,” wrote Morningstar Credit of 2 Washington Street. The special servicing notice stated that 286 of the building’s 345 rental units are designated by the City of New York to serve only as rentals for a maximum of 30 days, hence the hospitality-hybrid business model. That designation makes it unlikely the building can be turned into a multifamily property without zoning changes. Prior to Sonder’s bankruptcy, 2 Washington Street boasted short-term s
bullets: $131.5M CMBS loan backed by Moinian Group's 2 Washington Street (Manhattan) sent to special servicing; maturity Aug 2031.
Cash flow stress tied to master-lease tenant Sonder; short-term rental/hospitality hybrid units exposed to demand collapse.
Mixed-use asset with STR/hospitality dependency shows structural vulnerability; securitized debt at risk of extended special servicing.
stories.summary: A $131.5 million CMBS loan secured by Moinian Group's 2 Washington Street, a 483,000 sq ft, 22-story mixed-use complex in Lower Manhattan featuring short-term rental units, has been transferred to special servicing due to cash flow issues. The loan is split across BMARK 2021-B29, BMARK 2021-B28, and 3650R 2021-PF1 conduit deals (issued by Citigroup, BofA, and 3650 Capital in 2021) and matures August 6, 2031. The property's cash flow deterioration is attributed to Sonder, a global hospitality company that operated the master-lease and provided hybrid hotel-apartment units; Sonder's business decline has created vacancy and revenue pressure.
stories.impact: Moinian's 2 Washington Street distress signals structural weakness in hybrid STR/hospitality mixed-use models that became popular in 2018–2020. Sonder's underperformance (and eventual pivot away from short-term rental) has cascaded into lender loss and special servicing. For multifamily developers, this validates the long-standing underwriting caution against STR/hospitality-dependent revenue in mixed-use deals; residential-only or hotel-only are lower-volatility alternatives. The asset's mixed-use hybrid nature (upper floors residential, lower floors STR/hospitality) proved operationally complex and revenue-fragile when master-lease tenant failed. This reinforces the case for pure-play apartment development over mixed-use hybrids in high-cost urban markets.

stories.title: Dwight Capital Closes Two Corpus Christi Loans Totaling $96M
stories.excerpt: Dwight Capital has closed two HUD 223(f) refinance loans totaling $96MM for a pair of garden-style apartment communities in Corpus Christi, TX: A $48.2 million loan financed La Joya by Azali, a 336-unit waterfront co millionunity comprising 14 three-story residential buildings and an amenity and leasing building completed in 2015.  A $47.3 million loan financed Azali Heights (shown), a 312-unit community with 13 three-story residential buildings and an amenity and leasing building constructed in 2024. Both communities include one, two, and three-bedroom floor plans with private patios and balconies, as well as assortment of amenities including a business center, fitness center, pools and athletic courts and walking trails. Proceeds from both loans were used to retire existing debt, fund property enhancements, cover closing costs, and establish replacement reserve accounts for future capital improvements. Notably, the retired debt at La Joya by Azali included a bridge loan.  These transactions were originated by Andrew Tichy. The post Dwight Capital Closes Two Corpus Christi Loans Totaling $96M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Dwight Capital closes two HUD 223(f) refinance loans totaling $96M for Corpus Christi garden-style apartments.
La Joya by Azali: 336 units, 2015-built, $48.2M refi; Azali Heights: 312 units, 2024-built, $47.3M refi.
HUD 223(f) takeout financing available for multifamily; bridge debt retired; capital markets remain operational for seasoned/new stabilized assets.
stories.summary: Dwight Capital has closed two HUD 223(f) refinance loans totaling $96 million for two garden-style apartment communities in Corpus Christi, Texas. La Joya by Azali, a 336-unit community completed in 2015, received a $48.2 million loan; Azali Heights, a 312-unit community completed in 2024, received a $47.3 million loan. Both feature one-, two-, and three-bedroom units with patios/balconies and amenities (fitness, pools, courts, trails). Proceeds were used to retire existing debt (including a bridge loan at La Joya), fund property enhancements, and establish replacement reserves.
stories.impact: HUD 223(f) refi availability for Corpus Christi garden-style assets signals a functioning secondary market for stabilized multifamily in secondary-tier Texas markets. The fact that a newly completed (2024) Azali Heights community is already being refinanced (typical timing: 12–24 months post-stabilization) suggests strong operational performance and lender appetite for newer construction. For multifamily developers, this validates Corpus Christi as a market with institutional capital support and stable debt markets, reducing refinance risk on new development. HUD 223(f) programs remain a reliable off-ramp for acquisition/construction financing, particularly relevant given broader tightening in agency MBS and CMBS markets.

stories.title: ESD pitches $5B plan to complete Pacific Park
stories.excerpt: Pacific Park, the long delayed plan to cover railyards in a busy patch of Brooklyn, may finally move forward after Empire State Development and the project’s community development corporation board pitched a $5 billion buildout that would build more than 5,600 residential units, more than 1,200 of which would be income-restricted. The beleaguered project, first dubbed Atlantic Yards and still unfinished after more than two decades, originally called for $2.5 billion when former lead developer Bruce Ratner first announced it in 2003 alongside big names including late starchitect Frank Gehry and Brooklyn-born hip-hop star Jay-Z.  The Atlantic Yards Community Development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Empire State Development/Atlantic Yards CDC pitch $5B buildout to complete Pacific Park (Atlantic Yards) in Brooklyn.
Plan includes 5,600+ residential units (1,200+ income-restricted, 25% moderate/75% low income); 1,000 condo units; ~20 years post-announcement.
Zoning/entitlements progressing; union labor requirement; government backing implies tax incentives/public financing likely for affordability component.
stories.summary: Empire State Development and the Atlantic Yards Community Development Corporation have pitched a $5 billion buildout plan to complete Pacific Park (formerly Atlantic Yards) in Brooklyn. The project, originally announced by developer Bruce Ratner in 2003 with an initial $2.5 billion budget, would include over 5,600 residential units (1,200+ income-restricted, with 25% moderate-income and 75% low-income units across ~20 years development), 1,000 condo units, and mixed-use components. The development would deck over railyards along Atlantic Avenue and require union labor. Phase two zoning changes have progressed through initial approvals.
stories.impact: Pacific Park's re-energization after two decades of stagnation signals Brooklyn's continued institutional appeal and government commitment to large-scale, mixed-income development near transit (Barclays Center hub). The dramatic escalation from $2.5B (2003) to $5B (2026)—partly driven by inflation and affordability mandates—reflects cost inflation and regulatory/political pressure for deep affordable housing (75% low-income). For multifamily developers in Brooklyn, this validates the submarket's long-term demand resilience but also signals that new major projects will face affordability density requirements and extended entitlement timelines. The project's scale (5,600+ units) and public backing suggest it will absorb significant rental demand, creating potential competitive pressure on market-rate projects in proximate downtown Brooklyn submarkets (Fort Greene, Clinton Hill, Williamsburg). The reliance on government capital (TIF/PILOT/subsidies) for affordability will likely limit returns on 25%+ affordable deals, raising the bar for pure-market-rate alternative site selection.

stories.title: Dezer targets broader market with North Miami residential development
stories.excerpt: The Dezer father-son team is moving forward with Dezerland Park West after the proposed 586-unit project in North Miami was unanimously approved by the city’s planning board.  Sunny Isles Beach-based Dezer Development, led by Michael Dezer and son Gil Dezer, is planning a two-building residential development for the 4.44-acre site at 1890 Northeast 146th Street, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The site is currently used as a truck parking lot.  A 14-story building with 269 apartments and 4,500 square feet of retail space, plus an eight-story parking garage, will sit on the northern parcel of the site. An […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Dezer Development (North Miami-based) plans Dezerland Park West: 586-unit residential (269 + 317 units) on 4.44-acre industrial-zoned site.
Project includes retail, townhouses, parking; two-building design; 14-story + 8-story; units 500–1,500 SF; $24.5M in impact/permit fees.
Zoning overlay allows residential; transit-oriented district; planning board unanimously approved; construction target late 2028.
stories.summary: Dezer Development, led by Michael and Gil Dezer, has secured unanimous planning board approval for Dezerland Park West, a 586-unit residential development on a 4.44-acre site in North Miami (formerly used as truck parking lot) at 1890 Northeast 146th Street. The two-building design includes a 14-story building with 269 apartments and 4,500 sq ft of retail, plus an 8-story building with 317 apartments and 14 townhouses. Units range from 500–1,500 sq ft; project includes 20,000 sq ft of amenities. Dezer will pay $24.5 million in impact and permit fees and has committed to roadway improvements on 146th Street and 19th Avenue.
stories.impact: Dezer's shift from luxury-focused development (Sunny Isles Beach signature) to market-rate density in North Miami signals confidence in the broader South Florida rental market and willingness to underwrite mainstream middle-market demand. The industrial-zone-to-residential conversion via transit-oriented overlay reflects successful zoning evolution and reduced entitlement friction in North Miami. For multifamily developers, this validates North Miami as an emerging value node relative to saturated core Miami Beach/Wynwood and suggests demographic/job growth sufficient to support 586 units at market rates. The scale (586 units) and two-building efficiency suggest land costs and construction productivity are favorable relative to barrier-island alternatives, making North Miami a strategic acquisition target for regional/national developers seeking Florida density with lower land and entitlement cost.

stories.title: NYC developer in hot pursuit of Albany data center
stories.excerpt: Even as a data center moratorium sits on Gov. Kathy Hochul’s desk, awaiting a signature, a zoning change could push forward a development right in the state capital. EKG Group’s Michael-Henry Elghanian-Krayem is pushing to rezone into the “light industrial” classification at the Kenwood Commons site, the Times Union reported. The 76-acre site is zoned for a mixed-use campus today and needs numerous approvals to go forward, including at least two revisions to the city’s zoning regulations. The rezoning would allow for both a data center and a broader technology campus. The $570 million project consists of a 500,000-square-foot data […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: EKG Group (Michael-Henry Elghanian-Krayem) pursues zoning change for Kenwood Commons (Albany, NY) data center + tech campus.
76-acre mixed-use site; proposed rezone to 'light industrial'; $570M–$2B project: 500K SF data center, research labs, 300 apartments, retail.
Community opposition forming; energy/water concerns cited; affordable housing advocates push back; data center moratorium pending Gov. Hochul signature.
stories.summary: EKG Group, led by Michael-Henry Elghanian-Krayem, is pursuing a zoning change to reclassify the 76-acre Kenwood Commons site in Albany, New York, from mixed-use to 'light industrial' to enable development of a $570 million–$2 billion tech campus. The project includes a 500,000 sq ft data center, research labs, offices, 300 apartments, and ground-floor retail. Community opposition has formed around energy/water consumption and a push for affordable housing instead of a data center. A data center moratorium awaits Gov. Hochul's signature.
stories.impact: EKG's Albany tech campus proposal faces regulatory headwinds (state data center moratorium) and community opposition, but the inclusion of 300 apartments within a data center/tech campus is noteworthy—residential serves as a planning/zoning justification layer and workforce housing narrative. For multifamily developers, this signals that data center projects in growth-constrained regions (upstate NY) are increasingly bundled with residential to improve political feasibility and community acceptance. The 300-unit residential component is subordinate to the data center economics, but it indicates that pure tech/industrial projects in capacity-constrained markets may require residential co-development to win zoning and financing support. If the project proceeds, it could anchor residential demand in Albany and validate the submarket for follow-on apartment investment.

stories.title: Mystery bidder on Hawthorne Race Course facility could end planned “racino” development
stories.excerpt: An unknown corporate buyer represented by a shell company is set to purchase the Hawthorne Race Course in Stickney for $90 million, unless another buyer steps in to save the racetrack from likely being closed by the entity.  The suburban racetrack submitted a proposal in bankruptcy court to sell its assets to ALLIMAC 2023 LLC, according to the Chicago Tribune. Previously, Hawthorne president Tim Carey stated that proposals for the site’s redevelopment included data centers, a hot-button issue in city planning across the nation, as well as a $107 million offer. The track is the last remaining professional horse racing […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Hawthorne Race Course (Stickney, Chicago) in bankruptcy; mystery bidder (ALLIMAC 2023 LLC) submits $90M stalking horse offer.
Additional bids due June 30; prior redevelopment proposals included data centers and $107M offer; track is last Chicago-area racetrack.
Heavy industrial zoning, proximity to city attractive to alternative uses; 'racino' (casino hybrid) approved 2019 but never built.
stories.summary: Hawthorne Race Course in Stickney, Illinois, a suburban racetrack and the last professional horse racing venue in the Chicago area, is in Chapter 11 bankruptcy. An unknown bidder represented by shell company ALLIMAC 2023 LLC has submitted a $90 million stalking horse bid to purchase the track's assets. Bankruptcy judge Timothy Barnes will decide whether ALLIMAC has obtained stalking horse status on Monday, with additional bids due by Tuesday, June 30. Hilco Real Estate and Province have contacted ~8,000 potential buyers; 40 conducted due diligence. The track's heavy industrial zoning and city proximity have attracted interest for data center and other alternative-use proposals.
stories.impact: Hawthorne's forced liquidation via bankruptcy signals distressed real estate in the Chicago metro tied to obsolete single-use industrial assets. The track's heavy industrial zoning and urban edge location (Stickney, near major employment centers) make it attractive for adaptive reuse—data centers, logistics, or mixed-use development. For multifamily developers, Hawthorne represents a potential land acquisition opportunity in a high-barrier-to-entry Chicago submarket, but the bankruptcy process and likely litigation (multiple bidders, competing uses) will extend timeline. If acquired by a tech-focused buyer (data center), it could become an employment anchor driving residential demand in south-side Chicago; if converted to logistics/industrial, it will provide job growth and rental demand support for adjacent residential development.

stories.title: $580M cultural complex in Woodlawn snags Plan Commission approval
stories.excerpt: Around a half-decade after the Apostolic Church of God unveiled plans for the Woodlawn Central $580 million megadevelopment, key rezoning changes are giving an indication that the project is ready to move forward on the city’s South Side. The initial zoning change application for the 8-acre first phase space was filed in December 2025. The new change could allow the first phase, which includes a 14-story residential building with 104 units, to move forward. 22 of the units created would be affordable. The residential building would also include a 160-key hotel, 23,000 square feet of retail space, 3,500 square feet […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Apostolic Church of God/Woodlawn Central zoning change approved for $580M megaproject on Chicago South Side; Phase 1 rezoning filed Dec 2025.
Phase 1: 14-story residential building, 104 units (22 affordable), 160-key hotel, 23K SF retail, parking; permits targeted early 2027, completion late 2028.
Full build-out: ~1,000 affordable + market-rate + senior units, performance venue, restaurants; black entrepreneur-led; union labor; city South Side revitalization signal.
stories.summary: The Apostolic Church of God and Woodlawn Central development team have secured Chicago Plan Commission approval for zoning changes enabling Phase 1 of a $580 million cultural complex megadevelopment on the South Side at 6320 South Dorchester Avenue. Phase 1 includes a 14-story residential building with 104 units (22 affordable), a 160-key hotel, 23,000 sq ft of retail, 3,500 sq ft of commercial space, and parking. Developer J. Byron Brazier (son of Pastor Byron Brazier) aims to secure building permits in early 2027 and complete Phase 1 by late 2028. The full project envisions ~1,000 mixed-tenure units, performance venues, restaurants, and is billed as a cultural complex built by Black entrepreneurs.
stories.impact: Woodlawn Central's zoning approval and Phase 1 momentum signal institutional commitment to South Side Chicago revitalization anchored by cultural/mixed-use development. The project's scale (1,000+ units eventual), Black-led/church-backed ownership structure, and heavy affordability component (22 of 104 Phase 1 units) reflect both community demand and likely significant public subsidy/TIF capture. For multifamily developers, this validates Woodlawn (a historically disinvested South Side neighborhood) as an emerging target for institutional development capital, particularly if TIF and tax-increment financing remain available. The project will serve as a demand anchor and catalyst for adjacent residential infill, but the deep affordability mandate and union labor requirements will compress project economics and serve as a template for future South Side development. Market-rate developers in proximate South Side submarkets should model Woodlawn Central's completion (late 2028) as a potential demand driver and competitive supply surge.
